44	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker
45	
46	You compare **one Lean file** against **its blueprint chapter** and report whether the Lean follows the blueprint faithfully. You are read-only.
47	
48	## Your Job
49	
50	Your directive names one `.lean` file (e.g. `Algebra/WLocal.lean`) and its corresponding blueprint chapter (e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex`). You audit **bidirectionally**:
51	
52	### (A) Does the Lean follow the blueprint?
53	
54	For each `\lean{...}`-tagged block in the chapter, verify the Lean side:
55	
56	- **Declaration exists** with the right name (matches `\lean{namespace.theorem_name}`).
57	- **Signature matches** the informal statement in the chapter — the types are what the prose says they should be. A statement that type-checks but uses the wrong type class (e.g. `Smooth f` where the prose says "smooth of relative dimension n") is **wrong**, not "approximate".
58	- **Proof follows the blueprint sketch** — the proof in the Lean file should be a formalization of the steps in the chapter's `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}`, not a different argument that happens to produce the same conclusion.
59	- **No fake / placeholder statements** — definitions or theorems whose body is just `:= sorry` or a trivially-true tautology while the chapter prose claims something substantive.
60	- **No "temporary" definitions left in place** — comments like `-- TODO: replace with the real definition`, `-- placeholder until we figure out X`, or definitions whose body is suspicious (e.g. `def Foo := True`, weakened-wrong definitions standing in for the real concept).
61	- **No comments that explain away wrong code** — "we are using a wrong definition for now", "this proof is incomplete but will be fixed", etc. These are red flags, not acceptable workflow notes.
62	
63	### (B) Is the blueprint adequate for this Lean file?
64	
65	The blueprint can also be the failure mode. For the chapter you're reading, check:
66	
67	- **Every Lean declaration in this file should map to a blueprint block** (via `\lean{...}`) OR be an explicitly-helper-only declaration. If the file has substantive declarations the blueprint doesn't reference at all, the chapter is incomplete.
68	- **Proof sketches in the blueprint should be detailed enough** for a prover to formalize correctly. If you see Lean proofs that clearly went through significant reasoning the blueprint's `\begin{proof}` does NOT preview, flag the chapter as under-specified.
69	- **`\lean{...}` hints should pin the Lean target precisely.** If the blueprint says "smooth morphism" without specifying which Mathlib predicate (`Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n` vs `IsSmooth`), and the Lean file ended up with the wrong one, that's BOTH a Lean error AND a blueprint clarity failure. Flag both sides.
70	- **Definitions in the blueprint should be at the right level of generality** for the Lean to consume. If the blueprint defines something narrower than the project actually needs and the Lean had to write a parallel API to cover the gap, that's a blueprint adequacy failure.
71	
72	Both directions matter. Don't excuse a wrong Lean statement by saying "the blueprint is loose"; don't excuse a thin blueprint by saying "the Lean works anyway." Report both.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Checker Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Lean file
83	<path/to/file.lean>
84	
85	## Blueprint chapter
86	blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex
87	
88	## Known issues
89	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
90	```
91	
92	That's it. No strategy snapshot, no references, no full project context — just the two files and any pre-known issues to skip. Cross-file consistency is the job of the code-audit and blueprint-review subagents in the catalog; you stay focused on this one pair.
93	
94	## What you do
95	
96	1. Read your directive.
97	2. Read the Lean file completely. Note every declaration (`def`, `theorem`, `lemma`, `instance`, `axiom`, …) and its signature.
98	3. Read the blueprint chapter completely. List every `\lean{...}` reference and its corresponding declaration block.
99	4. **For each `\lean{...}` block**: locate the Lean declaration. Verify:
100	   - It exists.
101	   - Its signature matches the informal statement (use `archon-lean-lsp` for hover/signature info).
102	   - If the Lean has a body (proof or definition body), does it match the blueprint's prose? Naming and tactic choice can differ; the *mathematical content* should match.
103	5. **For each declaration in the Lean file**: is it `\lean{...}`-referenced from the blueprint? Unreferenced declarations are notable but not necessarily wrong (helpers may exist). Flag suspect ones.
104	6. **Scan for red flags** in the Lean file:
105	   - `:= sorry` where the blueprint claims a substantive statement (placeholder).
106	   - Suspect bodies like `:= True`, `:= rfl` for non-trivial claims, `:= Classical.choice _`.
107	   - Comments excusing wrong/incomplete code: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- will fix later`, `-- this is wrong but works for now`.
108	   - `axiom` declarations.
109	7. **Cross-check** any `import` paths and namespace usage that should match what the blueprint's `\lean{...}` hints suggest.
110	
111	You may use:
112	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics.
113	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to count sorries in the file.
114	
115	You may NOT modify the Lean file, the blueprint, or any other project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
116	
117	## Report format
118	
119	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
120	
121	```markdown
122	# Lean ↔ Blueprint Check Report
123	
124	## Slug
125	<slug>
126	
127	## Iteration
128	<NNN>
129	
130	## Files audited
131	- Lean: <path>
132	- Blueprint: <path>
133	
134	## Per-declaration
135	
136	For every `\lean{...}` block in the chapter, one entry:
137	
138	### `\lean{Foo.bar}` (chapter: \thm:bar)
139	- **Lean target exists**: yes / no (if no: declaration name in the .lean that comes closest, or "none found")
140	- **Signature matches**: yes / partial / no (with one line on the mismatch)
141	- **Proof follows sketch**: yes / partial / no / N/A (if N/A: chapter doesn't have a proof body to compare against)
142	- **notes**: <free-form, one line per observation>
143	
144	### `\lean{Foo.baz}` ...
145	
146	## Red flags
147	
148	(Section appears only if there are findings.)
149	
150	### Placeholder / suspect bodies
151	- `Foo.bar` at line 42: body is `:= sorry` but blueprint claims a substantive proof.
152	- `Foo.qux` at line 78: defined as `:= True`, suspect.
153	- ...
154	
155	### Excuse-comments
156	- `Foo.lean:113`: comment "TODO: replace with real def" attached to `Foo.frob`. Blueprint claims `Foo.frob` is the actual definition, not a placeholder.
157	- ...
158	
159	### Axioms / Classical.choice on non-trivial claims
160	- `Foo.lean:200`: `axiom` declaration `Foo.skip_step` introduced. Blueprint does not authorize this.
161	
162	## Unreferenced declarations (informational)
163	
164	<list of declarations in the Lean file that no `\lean{...}` references. Most are helpers — only flag the ones whose name suggests they should be in the blueprint.>
165	
166	## Blueprint adequacy for this file
167	
168	A bidirectional check: does the blueprint chapter give a prover enough detail to formalize this file correctly?
169	
170	- **Coverage**: <N>/<M> Lean declarations have a corresponding `\lean{...}` block in the chapter. Unreferenced declarations: <count> helpers (acceptable) + <count> substantive (flagged below).
171	- **Proof-sketch depth**: <one of: adequate / under-specified / silent>. If under-specified or silent, list which `\thm:...` blocks need expansion.
172	- **Hint precision**: <one of: precise / loose / wrong>. Loose hints (the prose pins one Mathlib predicate but the `\lean{...}` doesn't, leaving the prover to guess) get flagged. Wrong hints (the `\lean{...}` names a declaration whose signature doesn't match the prose) are critical.
173	- **Generality**: <one of: matches need / too narrow / too broad>. If too narrow, name the parallel API that the project ended up writing because the blueprint didn't cover the needed level of generality.
174	- **Recommended chapter-side actions**: <bullet list of items a blueprint-writing subagent should land if you flagged anything above>
175	
176	## Severity summary
177	
178	Apply these rules verbatim. Do NOT under-classify to soften the blow — the plan agent's gates depend on accurate severity, and a hidden critical finding hardens into shipped wrong code.
179	
180	- **must-fix-this-iter** — every one of the following lands here, no exceptions:
181	  - Placeholder body (`:= sorry`, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim, suspect `:= Classical.choice _` patterns) on a declaration the blueprint claims is substantive.
182	  - Signature mismatch with the blueprint's prose — including using the wrong Mathlib predicate (e.g. `Smooth` vs `SmoothOfRelativeDimension n`) when the prose pins one specific predicate.
183	  - Excuse-comments (`-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- temporary`, `-- placeholder`, `-- wrong but works for now`) on declarations the blueprint claims are real.
184	  - Axioms or `Classical.choice _` on substantive claims the blueprint does NOT authorize.
185	  - Blueprint adequacy failure: chapter is so under-specified that a prover could not have formalized this file correctly from prose alone.
186	  - Weakened-wrong definitions: Lean defines a structurally-different stand-in (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic Γ(X,⊤)` when the blueprint defines it as an invertible quasi-coherent O_X-module).
187	- **major** — partial signature mismatches that are fixable in-place, missing `\lean{...}` references to declarations that the blueprint should reference, comments that are stale but not actively misleading.
188	- **minor** — naming drift, helpers worth promoting to blueprint blocks, low-impact prose-vs-Lean differences.
189	
190	Overall verdict: one sentence.
191	```
192	
193	The plan agent's per-file gate (see plan.md and the blueprint-review subagent's `dispatcher_notes` in the catalog) treats every must-fix-this-iter finding as blocking. If you classify a wrong signature as "major" to avoid the gate firing, you are working against the project, not for it.
194	
195	## Return value
196	
197	Your final assistant message:
198	
199	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> declarations checked, <M> red flags`
200	- The path to your full report.
201	
202	## Reminders
203	
204	- **One file pair, no global context.** Cross-file consistency belongs to the catalog's code-audit and blueprint-review subagents.
205	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
206	- **Excuse-comments are red flags, not workflow.** Treat "we use a wrong def for now" with the suspicion it deserves.
207	- **Mathematical content match, not syntactic.** The Lean proof can choose different tactics than the chapter's sketch implies, as long as the mathematical steps are the same.

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.

44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116

121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.

127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.

131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.

137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.

Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 8	/-!
9	# Flattening stratification of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
10	
11	This file is the **A.2.a** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem — the technical heart that
14	turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
15	morphism `π : X ⟶ S` with coherent input `𝓕` into a finite collection of
16	locally-closed strata over which `𝓕` becomes flat.
17	
18	## Status (iter-176 Lane E file-skeleton — re-dispatch)
19	
20	iter-175 Lane E died to the Anthropic session-limit reset window without
21	ever calling `Write` (the file was never created). iter-176 re-dispatches
22	the file-skeleton verbatim. Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the
23	*intended* substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
24	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a
25	`sorry` body. The bodies are iter-177+ work; the substantive proofs are
26	deep (Nitsure~\S4 ~250-line argument: generic-flatness ⟶ Noetherian
27	induction ⟶ Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity ⟶ assembly via direct images
28	`E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)`).
29	
30	The 4 blueprint-pinned declarations are:
31	
32	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat` (def, ~8 LOC) — the
33	   **coherent-sheaf flatness predicate** for a coherent `𝓕` on `X` over
34	   `S` via `f : X ⟶ S`. Affine-locally: for `U ⊆ S` and `V ⊆ f⁻¹U` both
35	   affine, `Γ(𝓕, V)` is a flat `Γ(S, U)`-module via the composite
36	   `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V) → End(Γ(𝓕, V))`. The corresponding Stacks tag is
37	   00HB (module-level) lifted to a sheaf-level predicate by the
38	   affine-local descent characterisation.
39	
40	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem, ~15 LOC) — **generic
41	   flatness** [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base
42	   `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a
43	   non-empty open `V ⊆ S`.
44	
45	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification` (theorem, ~25 LOC) — the
46	   **flattening-stratification existence theorem** [Nitsure §4]: for `S`
47	   noetherian and `𝓕` coherent on a proper `π : X ⟶ S`, there exists a
48	   finite locally-closed stratification `{S_f}` of `S` (set-theoretically
49	   covering, pairwise disjoint) such that `𝓕` becomes flat on each
50	   stratum.
51	
52	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification_universal` (theorem, ~15 LOC)
53	   — the **universal property** of the stratification [Nitsure §4 (ii)]:
54	   any morphism `φ : T ⟶ S` such that `𝓕` pulled back to `X ×_S T` is
55	   `T`-flat factors uniquely through the stratum coproduct
56	   `i : ⨿ S_f ⟶ S`.
57	
58	Additionally, three sub-lemmas decompose the existence proof, plus a
59	curve-specialisation corollary feeding the Route~A consumer:
60	
61	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusStratification` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
62	   **special case `n = 0`** [Nitsure §4, n=0]: a coherent `𝓕` on `S`
63	   admits a finite locally-closed stratification by integer "rank"
64	   strata.
65	
66	6. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusReduction` (lemma, ~12 LOC) — the
67	   **Noetherian-induction reduction** [Nitsure §4 general case opening]:
68	   a finite reduced locally-closed stratification on which `𝓕`
69	   *already* becomes flat (without polynomial indexing).
70	
71	7. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatLocusAssembly` (lemma, ~15 LOC) — the
72	   **assembly step** [Nitsure §4 via direct images]: combining the
73	   `n = 0` stratification iteratively on the direct images
74	   `E_i = π_* 𝓕(N+i)` to refine to the Hilbert-polynomial-indexed
75	   stratification.
76	
77	8. `AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
78	   — the **relative-curve specialisation** for the Route~A consumer
79	   `C ×_k T → T`.
80	
81	## Note on type expressivity
82	
83	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
84	each declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
85	
86	- `CoherentSheafFlat` reduces to module-theoretic flatness of every
87	  affine-local section module over the corresponding affine-base ring
88	  via the `appLE` ring-hom — non-empty content because flatness of a
89	  module is the standard non-trivial homological condition.
90	- `genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V`
91	  and substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers
92	  are necessary (without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
93	- `flatteningStratification` packages a *finite* indexed family of
94	  *locally-closed-immersions* `ι : S_f ⟶ S` that pairwise *cover*
95	  `|S|` *disjointly* AND on which the pullback `𝓕|_{X ×_S S_f}` becomes
96	  `S_f`-flat — all four conjuncts are substantive (any single
97	  one trivialises if dropped).
98	- `flatteningStratification_universal` packages the universal property
99	  via `∃!` (unique existence of factorisation), a substantive Yoneda
100	  bijection content.
101

105	`AlgebraicGeometry.Flat` (morphism-level), `IsImmersion`,

135	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — algebraic generic freeness (finite case)
136	
137	This section builds the **module-theoretic** generic-freeness statements that
138	underlie the geometric `genericFlatness` (blueprint
139	`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4 "Lemma on Generic Flatness").
140	
141	The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
142	`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
143	is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
144	clearing denominators). Mathlib already supplies most of the dévissage
145	machinery (`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`,
146	`ModuleCat.free_shortExact`, `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`), but it does
147	**not** yet contain the polynomial-ring core (generic freeness for a finite
148	module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]`). See the file `task_results` handoff for the
149	precise decomposition of the remaining gap.
150	
151	What we *can* land axiom-clean here is the **finite-module / finite-morphism
152	case**: when `M` is finite as an `A`-module (in particular when `B` is
153	module-finite over `A`), generic freeness follows directly from
154	`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` applied at the
155	generic point `Frac A`, where `M ⊗_A Frac A` is a finite vector space hence
156	free. This is a genuine special case of the algebraic generic-freeness
157	theorem (the case of a *finite* morphism `X → S`), and a reusable building
158	block for the dévissage's leaves. -/
159	
160	namespace GenericFreeness
161	
162	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-module case.** For a noetherian integral domain
163	`A` and a finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that the
164	localisation `M_f` is free over `A_f = Localization.Away f`.
165	
166	This is the `d = 0` (finite-morphism) special case of the algebraic
167	generic-flatness theorem (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4):
168	inverting the generic point `Frac A`, the localised module is a finite vector
169	space hence free, and `Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers`
170	descends that freeness to a single basic open `D(f) ⊆ Spec A`. -/
171	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite
172	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
173	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
174	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
175	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
176	  haveI : Module.FinitePresentation A M := Module.finitePresentation_of_finite A M
177	  obtain ⟨r, hr, hfree, _⟩ :=
178	    Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers (nonZeroDivisors A)
179	      (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (nonZeroDivisors A) M) (FractionRing A)
180	  exact ⟨r, nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hr, hfree⟩
181	
182	/-- **Generic flatness, finite-module case.** The flatness form of
183	`exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite`: for a noetherian domain `A` and a
184	finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f` with `M_f` flat over `A_f`.
185	This is the affine-local content of `genericFlatness` for a finite morphism. -/
186	theorem exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite
187	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
188	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
189	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
190	      Module.Flat (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
191	  obtain ⟨f, hf, hfree⟩ := exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
192	  haveI := hfree
193	  exact ⟨f, hf, Module.Flat.of_free⟩
194	
195	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-morphism case.** If `A` is a noetherian domain,
196	`B` a *module-finite* `A`-algebra, and `M` a finite `B`-module (with the
197	compatible `A`-module structure), then there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` with `M_f`
198	free over `A_f`. Reduces to `exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite` via
199	`Module.Finite.trans` (a finite module over a module-finite algebra is finite
200	over the base). This is generic flatness for a *finite* morphism `X → S`. -/
201	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite
202	    (A : Type*) (B : Type*) (M : Type*)
203	    [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
204	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Module.Finite A B]
205	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
206	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
207	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
208	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
209	  haveI : Module.Finite A M := Module.Finite.trans B M
210	  exact exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
211	
212	end GenericFreeness

216	/-! ## §1. Coherent-sheaf flatness over a base
217	
218	For a morphism `f : X ⟶ S` of schemes and a coherent (more generally:
219	quasi-coherent) `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, the *coherent-sheaf flatness* predicate
220	`CoherentSheafFlat f 𝓕` captures Stacks 00HB lifted to the sheaf level:
221	for every point `x ∈ X` the stalk `𝓕_x` is a flat `𝓞_{S, f(x)}`-module
222	via the composite `𝓞_{S, f(x)} → 𝓞_{X, x} → End(𝓕_x)`.
223	
224	Affine-locally (Stacks 00HB equivalence): on an affine open `U = Spec A
225	⊆ S` and an affine open `V = Spec B ⊆ f⁻¹U` with `𝓕|_V = M̃` for a
226	finite `B`-module `M`, the condition becomes `Module.Flat A M` with the
227	`A`-module structure inherited via the ring map `A → B`. The
228	file-internal encoding uses this affine-local form.
229	
230	Blueprint reference: `def:coherent_sheaf_flat`. -/
231	
232	/-- A coherent sheaf `𝓕` on `X` is **flat over `S`** for a morphism
233	`f : X ⟶ S` if, for every affine open `U ⊆ S` and every affine open
234	`V ⊆ f⁻¹U`, the `𝓞_X`-module of sections `Γ(𝓕, V)` is flat as a
235	`Γ(S, U)`-module, where the `Γ(S, U)`-action is the restriction of
236	scalars along the ring morphism `Γ(S, U) → Γ(X, V)` (the `appLE`
237	morphism of `f`).
238	
239	This is the affine-local form of Stacks 00HB (a coherent sheaf is flat
240	over the base if every stalk is flat over the corresponding base
241	stalk); the affine-local form and stalk form are equivalent for
242	*quasi-coherent* sheaves on a noetherian base, which is the regime of
243	the flattening-stratification theorem.
244	
245	iter-177+: the body of subsequent flat-locus theorems may package this
246	predicate as an instance-driven typeclass `Module.Flat`-style if the
247	sheafified-tensor infrastructure lands in Mathlib; for the iter-176
248	file-skeleton it is encoded as a `Prop` quantifier. -/
249	def CoherentSheafFlat {X S : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ S) (F : X.Modules) : Prop :=
250	  ∀ {U : S.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen U) {V : X.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen V)
251	    (e : V ≤ f ⁻¹ᵁ U),
252	    letI : Module Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V) := Module.compHom _ (f.appLE U V e).hom
253	    Module.Flat Γ(S, U) Γ(F, V)
254	
255	end Scheme
256	
257	/-! ## §2. Generic flatness (Nitsure §4)
258	
259	Over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf on a finite-type
260	`X ⟶ S` is flat above some non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. This is the inductive
261	engine of the flattening-stratification theorem: combined with
262	Noetherian induction on the closed complement `S ∖ V`, it produces the
263	finite stratification of `S` by flatness loci.
264	
265	Algebraically (theorem `generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin): for a
266	noetherian domain `A`, a finite-type `A`-algebra `B`, and a finite
267	`B`-module `M`, there exists a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that `M_f` is a
268	free `A_f`-module. The geometric form (this declaration) restricts to a
269	non-empty affine open `Spec A ⊆ S` and applies the algebraic form on
270	each finite-type-algebra patch of `X` above `Spec A`.
271	
272	Blueprint reference: `thm:generic_flatness` (Nitsure §4). -/
273	
274	/-- **Generic flatness theorem** (Nitsure §4 / Stacks ?).
275	
276	For a noetherian integral scheme `S`, a finite-type morphism `p : X ⟶ S`,
277	and a coherent `𝓞_X`-module `𝓕`, there exists a non-empty open subscheme
278	`V ⊆ S` such that `𝓕|_{X_V} = 𝓕|_{p⁻¹V}` is flat over `𝓞_V`.
279	
280	iter-177+: the body follows Nitsure §4: pass to a non-empty affine open
281	`Spec A ⊆ S` where `A` is a noetherian domain, then apply the algebraic
282	form (Noether normalisation + Auslander–Buchsbaum-style filtration
283	argument) to each finite-type-`A`-algebra `B` arising from an affine
284	cover of `p⁻¹(Spec A)`. The witness `V` is the common basic open
285	`D(f_1 f_2 ⋯ f_r) ⊆ Spec A` clearing the finitely many
286	generic-flatness elements `f_i ∈ A` produced on each patch. -/
287	theorem genericFlatness {S X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral S] [IsLocallyNoetherian S]
288	    (p : X ⟶ S) [LocallyOfFiniteType p] (F : X.Modules) :
289	    ∃ (V : S.Opens), (V : Set S).Nonempty ∧
290	      ∀ {U : S.Opens} (_ : IsAffineOpen U) (_ : U ≤ V) {W : X.Opens}
291	        (_ : IsAffineOpen W) (e : W ≤ p ⁻¹ᵁ U),
292	        letI : Module Γ(S, U) Γ(F, W) := Module.compHom _ (p.appLE U W e).hom

7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that

10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master

12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter

20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.genericFlatnessAlgebraic}
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent

291	  \(n = 0\), assembled over the relative twists \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\).  As the
292	  argument is local on \(S\) (the strata glue by their universal property, part
293	  (ii)), and as the three conclusions are proved through the sub-lemmas below,
294	  we assemble them in the order (i), (ii), (iii).

296	  \emph{Part (i): finiteness of the Hilbert-polynomial set.}
297	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}, Noetherian induction on \(S\) (whose
298	  inductive step invokes generic flatness, Theorem~\ref{thm:generic_flatness},
299	  on the integral locally-closed strata cut out by the irreducible-component
300	  decomposition of \(S\)) produces finitely many reduced, pairwise disjoint,
301	  locally-closed subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\) set-theoretically covering
302	  \(|S|\) on each of which \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) in
303	  the sense of Definition~\ref{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}.  On a flat family the
304	  Hilbert polynomial \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is locally constant, so
305	  it takes finitely many values on each quasi-compact \(V_i\); the finite union
306	  over the finitely many \(V_i\) shows that the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
307	  occurring on \(S\) is finite.
308	
309	  \emph{Part (ii): existence of the strata and their universal property.}
310	  Working over the flat pieces \(V_i\) of part (i), semicontinuity
311	  (cohomology-and-base-change) yields a uniform integer \(N\) such that, for all
312	  \(s \in S\) and \(m \ge N\), the higher cohomologies \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\)
313	  vanish for \(r \ge 1\) and the base-change homomorphism
314	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism.  With this
315	  \(N\) fixed, Lemma~\ref{lem:noetherian_induction_strata} applies the \(n = 0\)
316	  stratification mechanism (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_locus_open}) successively to the
317	  coherent sheaves \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\),
318	  refining stratum by stratum.  After \(n+1\) steps this produces a finite
319	  family of locally-closed subschemes \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n} \subseteq S\),
320	  universal for the property that each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free sheaf
321	  of constant rank \(e_i\); re-indexing the tuple \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
322	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\)

324	  isomorphism and the cohomology vanishing force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has
325	  degree \(\le n\) and is determined by its values at \(N, \dots, N+n\)), so
326	  \(|W_f| = \{s : P_s = f\}\); these subsets are disjoint with union \(|S|\).

328	  subscheme cut out by the further local-freeness conditions

330	  \(i \ge 0\)'' (a chain of coherent ideal sheaves that stabilises by
331	  Noetherianity), and \(\F|_{\PP^n_{S_f}}\) is flat over \(S_f\)

333	  \(\bigoplus_{i \gg 0} \pi_* \F(i)|_{S_f}\) is locally free.  Forming

336	  \(\varphi^* \F\) flat over \(T\) has locally constant Hilbert polynomial, hence

341	  uniquely determined by \(f\).

557	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: full proof is the long body of [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem
558	% ``flattening stratification'', spanning L1844-L2090 (~250 lines: special
559	% case, Noetherian induction, statements (A)/(B)/(C), then the assembly via

563	\begin{proof}
564	  
565	  By \cref{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}, the family of Hilbert polynomials
566	  \(\{P_s\}\) on \(S\) is finite, and there is a finite stratification
567	  \(\{V_i\}\) of \(S\) on each of which \(\F\) is flat with locally-constant
568	  Hilbert polynomial.  Apply semi-continuity (cohomology and base change,
569	  [Hartshorne], III.12 / [Stacks Project], tag 02KH for the \(H^0\)-version) on
570	  the noetherian \(V_i\) to obtain a uniform integer \(N_1\) with
571	  \(R^r \pi_* \F(m) = 0\) for \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\), and \(H^r(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m)) = 0\)
572	  for all \(s \in S\), \(r \ge 1\), \(m \ge N_1\) (statement (B) of Nitsure's proof).
573	  Choose \(r_i \ge N_1\) on each \(V_i\) such that the base-change comparison
574	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_{V_i} \to (\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m)\) is an isomorphism for
575	  \(m \ge r_i\); composing with the cohomology-and-base-change isomorphism
576	  \(((\pi_i)_* \F_{V_i}(m))|_s \xrightarrow{\sim} H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) for
577	  the flat family on \(V_i\) yields uniform \(N = \max r_i\) such that
578	  \((\pi_* \F(m))|_s \to H^0(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) is an isomorphism for \(m \ge N\)
579	  and all \(s \in S\) (statement (C)).
580	
581	  Apply \cref{lem:flat_locus_open} sequentially to the coherent sheaves
582	  \(E_i = \pi_* \F(N+i)\) on \(S\) for \(i = 0, \dots, n\): \(E_0\) produces a
583	  stratification \(\{W_{e_0}\}\) of \(S\); on each \(W_{e_0}\) apply the lemma to
584	  \(E_1|_{W_{e_0}}\) to refine to \(\{W_{e_0, e_1}\}\), and continue. After \(n+1\)
585	  steps the \(W_{e_0, \dots, e_n}\) form a finite locally-closed stratification
586	  of \(S\) universal for the property ``each \(E_i\) pulls back to a locally-free
587	  \(\OO_T\)-module of constant rank \(e_i\)''.  Re-index \((e_0, \dots, e_n)\) by the
588	  unique numerical polynomial \(f\) of degree \(\le n\) with \(f(N+i) = e_i\) to get
589	  the family \(\{W_f\}\) indexed by polynomials.  At any \(s \in W_f\), statement
590	  (C) and vanishing (B) force \(P_s = f\) (since \(P_s\) has degree \(\le n\) and is
591	  determined by its values at \(N, N+1, \dots, N+n\)).
592	
593	  The Hilbert-polynomial-coincidence locus \(|W_f|\) may have non-flat scheme
594	  structure; refine to \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) by intersecting with the
595	  closed-subscheme conditions ``\(\pi_* \F(N+i)|_{W_f}\) is locally-free of rank
596	  \(f(N+i)\)'' for all \(i \ge 0\).  These conditions are cut out by an increasing
597	  chain of coherent ideal sheaves \(I_0 \subseteq I_0 + I_1 \subseteq \cdots\) on
598	  \(W_f\); Noetherianity terminates the chain in finitely many steps, defining a
599	  coherent ideal sheaf \(I\) and a closed subscheme \(S_f \subseteq W_f\) with
600	  \(|S_f| = |W_f|\) on which \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) is locally-free of rank \(f(N+i)\)
601	  for all \(i \ge 0\). The base-change-without-flatness lemma (cf.\ [Nitsure],
602	  \S3) yields some \(N' \ge N\) such that for \(i \ge N'\),
603	  \((\pi_* \F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism, hence
604	  the graded \(A\)-module \(\bigoplus_{i \ge N'} (\pi_{S_f})_* \F_{S_f}(i)\) is
605	  locally-free on \(S_f\), hence \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\). The
606	  closure-of-strata property (iii) reduces to the corresponding property in
607	  the \(n=0\) case for \(\pi_* \F(p)\) on \(S\) at any \(p \ge N\) separating all
608	  Hilbert polynomials.
609	\end{proof}

701	  % universal property

703	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
704	  % "\textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
705	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
706	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
707	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
708	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
709	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
710	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
711	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
712	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\)."
713	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (part (ii) of the main theorem); cf.\ [Stacks
714	  Project], tag 052H.}
715	  Let \(S\), \(\F\), and \(\{S_f\}_{f \in I}\) be as in
716	  \cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}.  Set
717	  \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) with the coproduct morphism
718	  \(i : S' \to S\) assembled from the locally-closed inclusions.  Then \(i^* \F\)
719	  is flat over \(S'\), and the pair \((S', i)\) is universal: for any morphism

721	  Noetherian hypothesis on \(S\) being preserved), the pullback
722	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is \(T\)-flat if and only if \(\varphi\) factors

728	% By Lemma \ref{base change without flatness},
729	% there exists some \(N' \ge N\) such that for all \(i\ge N'\),
730	% the base-change
731	% \((\pi_*\F(i))|_{S_f} \to (\pi_{S_f})_*\F_{S_f}(i)\) is an isomorphism for
732	% each \(S_f\). Therefore
733	% \(\F_{S_f}\) is flat over \(S_f\) by Lemma \ref{graded module is flat},
734	% as the direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N'\) are locally
735	% free over \(S_f\). Conversely, if \(\phi : T\to S\) is a morphism such
736	% that \(\F_T\) is flat, then the Hilbert polynomial is locally
737	% constant over \(T\). Let \(T_f\) be the open and closed subscheme of \(T\)
738	% where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\). Clearly, the set
739	% map \(|T_f| \to |S|\) factors via \(|S_f|\). But as the direct images
740	% \({\pi_T}_*\F_T(i)\) are locally free of rank \(f(i)\) on \(T_f\), it
741	% follows in fact that the schematic morphism \(T_f \to S\) factors
742	% via \(S_f\), proving the property (ii) of the theorem."

744	\begin{proof}
745	  
746	  Flatness of \(i^* \F\) over \(S'\) was established at the end of the proof of
747	  \cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} (the
748	  base-change-without-flatness lemma plus the graded-module-flatness criterion
749	  applied to the coherent sheaves \(\pi_* \F(N+i)\) on each \(S_f\)).
750	  Conversely, if \(\varphi : T \to S\) satisfies \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\) is
751	  \(T\)-flat, then \(T\)-flatness of the family forces the Hilbert polynomial to be
752	  locally constant on \(T\).  Decompose \(T = \coprod_f T_f\) into the open-closed
753	  subschemes where the Hilbert polynomial is \(f\).  The set map \(|T_f| \to |S|\)

759	  morphism \(T_f \to S_f\) extending the set map. Assembling over \(f\) gives the

763	\section{Application: relative curves}
764	\label{sec:flatstrat_curve_application}
765	
766	In the Route~A consumer setting, the input to the flattening-stratification
767	machinery is the pullback \(C \times_k T \to T\) where \(C\) is a smooth proper
768	curve over a field \(k\) and \(T\) is any \(k\)-scheme.  The hypotheses of
769	Nitsure's \cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} demand
770	\emph{projective} relative dimension, not merely \emph{proper}; we record that
771	no extra work is required to instantiate the theorem in our setting.
772	
773	\begin{lemma}
774	[Smooth proper curve over \(k\) is projective]
775	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}
776	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.TODO.smoothProperCurveProjective}
777	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6 (curve \(\Rightarrow\) quasi-projective;
778	  % proper \(\Rightarrow\) projective for curves), and standard consequences in
779	  % chapter III. The precise statement ``every smooth proper curve over a field
780	  % is projective'' is one of the classical equivalences for \(\dim 1\) and is
781	  % textbook material; this block is project-bespoke (no verbatim source
782	  % quote is reproduced here as the result is folklore-level and cited only
783	  % structurally for the Route~A reduction).
784	  \textit{Source: classical; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.6.7 \& II.7.6 (curves).}
785	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\).  Then \(C \to \Spec k\) is
786	  projective.  Consequently, for any noetherian \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the relative
787	  curve \(C \times_k T \to T\) is projective in the sense of Grothendieck (there
788	  exists a relatively very ample line bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\)).
789	\end{lemma}
790	
791	\begin{proof}
792	  Projectivity of a smooth proper curve over a field is classical
793	  ([Hartshorne], II.6.7 / II.7.6; alternatively: every divisor of large
794	  positive degree on a smooth proper curve \(C\) is very ample, providing the
795	  embedding \(C \hookrightarrow \PP^N_k\) for some \(N\)). Pulling the ample line
796	  bundle back along \(C \times_k T \to C\) produces a relatively very ample line
797	  bundle on \(C \times_k T\) over \(T\).
798	\end{proof}
799	
800	\begin{corollary}
801	
802	\leanok
803	[Flattening stratification for a coherent sheaf on a relative curve]
804	  \label{cor:flattening_stratification_curve}
805	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification.ofCurve}
806	  \uses{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective, thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
807	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(C\) a smooth proper curve over \(k\), and \(T\) a noetherian
808	  \(k\)-scheme.  Let \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on \(C \times_k T\). Then the
809	  flattening-stratification conclusion of
810	  \cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} applies: there is a finite
811	  set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials and locally-closed subschemes
812	  \(T_f \hookrightarrow T\) for \(f \in I\), set-theoretically covering \(T\)
813	  disjointly, such that (a) \(\F|_{C \times_k T_f}\) is flat over \(T_f\) for
814	  each \(f\), and (b) any morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\) such that
815	  \(\varphi^* \F\) on \(C \times_k T'\) is \(T'\)-flat factors uniquely through some
816	  \(T_f\).
817	\end{corollary}
818	
819	\begin{proof}
820	  
821	  By \cref{lem:smooth_proper_curve_projective}, \(C \times_k T \to T\) is
822	  projective, hence factors through a closed immersion into \(\PP^n_T\) for some
823	  \(n \ge 0\).  Push \(\F\) forward along the closed immersion (extending by zero
824	  outside the image) to a coherent sheaf \(\tilde \F\) on \(\PP^n_T\); this
825	  operation preserves \(T\)-flatness in both directions because the closed
826	  immersion is a finite morphism.  Apply
827	  \cref{thm:flattening_stratification_exists} to \(\tilde \F\) on
828	  \(\PP^n_T\) to obtain \(\{T_f\}\) with the stated universal property.
829	\end{proof}
